cognates.correspondence_path: Ar ق-ل-ب ↔ He ל-ב (metathesis/loss of initial q is often posited, though debated).
cognates.notes: Hebrew 'lev' (l-b) lacks the initial q-reflex seen in other Semitic languages, potentially due to historical root-shortening or a distinct Proto-Semitic variation.

cognates.correspondence_path: Ar ق ↔ Syc ∅; Ar ל ↔ Syc ל; Ar ب ↔ Syc ب — semantic match is strong, but initial consonant mismatch renders this likely a distinct formation or early loss in Aramaic branch.
cognates.notes: The Aramaic/Syriac form often displays a loss of the root-initial element associated with the Arabic 'q-l-b' form.

caveats: v0 prototype: this is an LLM-generated analysis, not a lexicon lookup. Results may be incomplete or incorrect, especially for Syriac, Tigrinya, and rare forms. Verify against Wiktionary or a scholarly lexicon before citing.

The root q-l-b 'heart' is specifically Arabic. While Hebrew/Aramaic 'l-b' shares the semantic field and the l-b consonants, the initial 'q' in Arabic is not universally accepted as a cognate component in some historical linguistic models; it may represent a divergent root formation.
Arabic 'qalb' refers to the heart; note that in some contexts in modern Arabic it can also mean 'turning/reversing' (root q-l-b, which is a different lexeme with the same spelling).
